items.q: When water boils, what is broken?
items.correct: Intermolecular forces (hydrogen bonds) between water molecules are broken — the O-H covalent bonds stay intact.
items.misconception: The covalent bonds between hydrogen and oxygen atoms are broken when water boils.
items.random_wrong: Water molecules split into hydrogen and oxygen gas when they boil.
items.why: Students don't distinguish between bonds within a molecule and forces between molecules, so they assume boiling 'breaks the molecule apart'.

items.q: Why does first ionisation energy generally increase across a period?
items.correct: Nuclear charge increases while shielding stays roughly constant, so electrons are held more tightly.
items.misconception: Atoms get bigger across a period, making electrons easier to add but harder to remove.
items.random_wrong: Atoms across a period have more shells, pulling electrons closer.
items.why: Students confuse the direction of the atomic-radius trend across a period (it actually decreases) and use wrong size reasoning.

items.q: At room temperature and pressure, what volume does 1 mole of any gas occupy?
items.correct: Approximately 24 dm^3 (24,000 cm^3), regardless of the identity of the gas.
items.misconception: Heavier gases occupy a smaller volume per mole because their particles are denser.
items.random_wrong: Different gases occupy completely different volumes proportional to their molar mass.
items.why: Students assume heavier particles must be more compact, missing that gas volume depends only on number of particles, T and P (Avogadro's law).
Correct answer: fractional distillation. “filtration” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: petrol/gas. “bitumen” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: CO2 makes up approximately what % of today's atmosphere?
items.why: The real figure is 0.04. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
